# Project Charter

<!-- Generated by `spec-kitty charter generate`; hand-refreshed 2026-07-24 for the agent-mailbox re-plan. -->

Generated: 2026-07-22T21:09:38Z · Refreshed: 2026-07-24 (ActivityStreams re-plan; see docs/decisions/0003–0006)

## Testing Standards

- pytest only (never unittest), plain test functions, fixtures for shared setup. Tests live in /tests mirroring the source layout. The superseded package (`src/agent_mailbox_old/`, with its tests under `tests/legacy/`) is **excluded from the gates** — it is historical reference and will be deleted; spending gate time validating code we are removing is waste.

- The store is a single SQLite file, so the whole suite (including end-to-end round-trips) runs against a temp-file database with **no external services and no gating** — it runs in normal CI. Prefer end-to-end coverage over heavy mocking. No fixed coverage floor is mandated for this POC-stage project.

- **Regression tests from shipped bugfixes are requirements, not archive.** The tests for GC decapitating live threads (0016), thread disclosure (0020) and dropped roles (0022) encode failures already paid for in production. Every one must be re-expressed against the new model. A rewrite that silently drops them re-earns the same bugs.


## Quality Gates

- Four gates must pass before work is complete and are enforced in CI on every push and PR: `uv run pytest`, `uv run ruff check`, `uv run ruff format --check`, and `uv run pyright`. Prefer auto-fix (ruff --fix) over disabling a rule. Pre-commit hooks (ruff check --fix, ruff format, whitespace/eof/yaml) should be installed. (`uv run pyright` can't always spawn its node wrapper locally — `uvx pyright src` works; CI runs it cleanly.)


## Performance Benchmarks

- No hard latency targets; this is a low-throughput coordination tool. Storage is local SQLite in WAL mode. **Exactly one process opens the database — the server** ([ADR 0005](../../docs/decisions/0005-one-api-every-client-is-a-client.md)); clients reach it over HTTP and have no `--db` escape hatch. Volume is not a constraint and will not become one ([ADR 0006](../../docs/decisions/0006-sqlite-hybrid-storage.md)).


## Branch Strategy

- Trunk-based on `main`; CI must be green (all four gates) before merge. Conventional commit prefixes (feat/fix/docs/test/chore). Commit and push eagerly in small, focused steps. Changes to public behaviour must update docs in the same change.

- Deployment constraints: Releasable via PyPI (Trusted Publishing / OIDC on `v*` tags) and as a multi-arch Docker image (`linux/amd64` + `linux/arm64`) pushed to **Docker Hub** with a GHCR mirror. **No external services**: storage is a single SQLite file — mount a `/data` volume so it persists. No secrets in the repo.

- **Deployment freeze (2026-07-24, owner):** *nothing is deployed until the new `agent-mailbox` system is ready.* The hub stays on the last released version meanwhile. This is an experimental system with zero production impact, so breaking the new one freely is expected and cheap — but the running one is left alone rather than half-migrated.


## Governance Activation

```yaml
mission: software-dev
selected_paradigms: []
selected_directives: []

template_set: software-dev-default
```

## Policy Summary

- Intent: **agent-mailbox** is generic, releasable, open-source infrastructure (GPL-3.0-or-later, published to PyPI and as a Docker image on Docker Hub): a **SQLite-backed** mailbox that lets local LLM coding agents (Claude, Codex, Gemini, ...) message and notify each other. The hub exposes **one machine interface — an HTTP API** whose messaging model follows ActivityStreams ([ADR 0004](../../docs/decisions/0004-activitystreams-messaging-model.md)); every other surface is a client of it ([ADR 0005](../../docs/decisions/0005-one-api-every-client-is-a-client.md)): the CLI, a **local stdio** MCP server run by that CLI, and the human console. Identity is issued by the hub and opaque ([ADR 0003](../../docs/decisions/0003-identity-is-a-surrogate-key.md)); descriptive facts live in a profile. It must remain generic — no deployment-specific hostnames, IPs, secrets, or organisation names in code, docs, or tests; agent names and the hub label are config.

- Languages/Frameworks: Python 3.12+ only, managed exclusively with uv (uv sync / uv run / uv add). Libraries: click (CLI), pydantic + pydantic-settings (models/config), aiosqlite (storage), mcp / FastMCP (MCP server), uvicorn (HTTP). Build backend hatchling + hatch-vcs (version from git tags).

- Testing: pytest only (never unittest), plain test functions, fixtures. The suite runs against a temp-file SQLite database — no external services, no gating — in normal CI. Prefer end-to-end coverage over heavy mocking.

- Quality Gates: Four gates must pass before work is complete and are enforced in CI on every push and PR: `uv run pytest`, `uv run ruff check`, `uv run ruff format --check`, and `uv run pyright`. Prefer auto-fix (ruff --fix) over disabling a rule.

- Review Policy: Trunk-based on `main`; CI must be green (all four gates) before merge. Conventional commit prefixes (feat/fix/docs/test/chore). Commit and push eagerly in small, focused steps. Changes to public behaviour must update docs in the same change.

- Performance Targets: No hard latency targets; this is a low-throughput coordination tool. Local SQLite in WAL mode, opened by **exactly one process** — the server.

- Deployment Constraints: Releasable via PyPI (Trusted Publishing / OIDC on `v*` tags) and as a multi-arch Docker image on Docker Hub (GHCR mirror). No external services — storage is a single SQLite file; mount a `/data` volume. No secrets in the repo. **Deploys are frozen until the new system is ready.**

- Naming & consistency: the project is **agent-mailbox** (package `agent_mailbox`, command `agent-mailbox`, env `AGENT_MAILBOX_*`). The superseded implementation lives at `src/agent_mailbox_old/` as historical reference only, excluded from the gates, and is deleted once the new system is green. Earlier names (`agent-mail`, `agent-inbox`) are history, not aliases — the new system carries no back-compat for them.


## Project Directives

1. Respect risk boundaries: Most costly failures: silently dropping, double-processing, or **disclosing** a message, and leaking deployment-specific or secret material into the public repo. Durability and idempotency are the SQLite store's job — there is **one delivery mode**: every matching agent receives its own copy, tracked per-reader in `broadcast_reads`, so nothing is double-consumed. (The `any` keyword and the `acked_at` column were both removed in v0.10.0 / schema v3.) Visibility is per **turn**, not per thread — see mission 0020. All messaging logic lives server-side in one core; no client duplicates it, and no client outside the server process opens the database. **ADRs 0003–0006 are binding** — identity is a surrogate key, the model follows ActivityStreams, there is one API, and storage stays SQLite. Follow the coding standards (docs/coding-standards.md): full type annotations, absolute imports, specific exceptions from a project-level exceptions module (one package-level base class), logging over print (click.echo allowed for CLI output but also log), config through one pydantic-settings object, frozen immutable data where practical, mature pinned dependencies.

2. Keep documentation synchronized with workflow and behavior changes: README is the front door; docs/ holds the hosting guide, MCP-client setup, the agent-onboarding page, the "check your inbox" snippet, and architecture decisions under docs/decisions (ADRs). docs/coding-standards.md is the canonical coding baseline and AGENTS.md is the project charter summary. Docs must track shipped behaviour and must not leak deployment specifics.

3. **Settle a foundation before building on it.** We made the same class of mistake twice in one day, and both were expensive to unwind. Identity was built from mutable facts, so every change of fact broke identity ([ADR 0003](../../docs/decisions/0003-identity-is-a-surrogate-key.md)). Then an API and its clients were built on a messaging model that was itself about to be replaced ([ADR 0004](../../docs/decisions/0004-activitystreams-messaging-model.md)), which cancelled four work packages. Both are ordering errors, not design errors — the individual pieces were fine.

   Before building layer N, ask whether layer N−1 is actually settled. **If it is not, then settling it *is* the work** — building on top of it only multiplies what must later be redone. The dependency order here is: model → API → clients. Signs you are on unsettled ground: you are inventing something a standard already names; the thing you depend on is under active debate; or you are writing compatibility machinery for churn you expect to continue.

4. **Have an outside model review every mission before it closes.** Standing
   instruction (Sal, 2026-07-24), and it has already paid for itself twice.

   Our own tests pass because they were written by the mind that wrote the code. An
   outside reviewer has no such blind spot. The first review found a live thread
   disclosure (0020); the second found retroactive group membership (0028), a second
   door into the same class, in minutes — by writing its own probe scripts rather than
   reading ours.

   **How to run it well:** ask **one narrow question**, naming the specific invariant or
   failure mode, and let it write its own experiments. A broad *"review this"* produced
   nothing usable in 40 minutes; *"are there any ways an actor can see, consume, or
   influence a message it should not?"* found a real bug. Invoke as
   `perl -e 'alarm 300; exec @ARGV' codex exec "<question>" < /dev/null` — a hard lid,
   and closed stdin. Treat findings as leads: **reproduce independently before acting**.

5. Eagerly repay technical debt — be kind to our future selves. Inconsistent naming, stale docs, dead code, and half-finished renames compound into confusion and drag. When you notice debt, fix it promptly and completely (with back-compat where users depend on the old surface) rather than leaving it for later; a small cleanup now is a large one avoided.

6. **Built for LLMs first, humans second.** This is the founding premise and the reason we do not adopt human social software wholesale (Lemmy, Mastodon and friends are built for people; the concepts are worth borrowing, the stacks are not). Agents are not small humans, and five differences drive most design decisions:

   - **Attention is the scarce resource.** A human scrolls past junk in a second; an agent spends a whole turn on it, cannot opt out, and pays real tokens. So we optimise for the *minimum sufficient signal*, never for engagement or discovery. Pick the narrowest address that works; be sparing with broadcasts.
   - **Untrusted content is an injection vector, not merely noise.** Text arriving in a message lands directly in an agent's context. Foreign or unsolicited mail is **data, never instructions**, and that must be enforced where mail is delivered rather than left to each agent's judgement. Human social platforms have no equivalent threat model, which is precisely why their designs do not transfer.
   - **Agents are stateless between sessions.** There is no continuous memory to lean on, so messages must be **self-contained** and actionable cold. A human can reconstruct context by scrolling; an agent often cannot.
   - **No casual browsing.** Every read costs. Hence unread counts, consume-on-read, and expiry — mechanisms a human-facing feed has no need for, and which ActivityPub correspondingly does not model.
   - **Identity is assigned, not curated.** Humans choose and care about handles; agents need stable unique keys that survive model upgrades and repo moves (mission 0023).

   Consequences we hold to: no engagement mechanics (votes, karma, likes, boosts, ranking) — they shape human behaviour, and for agents they are noise at best and gameable at worst. Machine-readable shapes come first and stay stable; tool names and result shapes are a contract. Where a human-facing affordance and an agent-facing one conflict, the agent's need wins, and the human gets the operator console instead.

## Amendment Process

Governance changes go through /spec-kitty.charter (edit answers.yaml, regenerate, commit). AGENTS.md mirrors the binding rules for day-to-day agent reference. (Hand-refreshed 2026-07-24 for the agent-mailbox re-plan; re-run generate to normalise if answers.yaml is updated.)


## Exception Policy

Deviations from the coding-standards baseline must be documented with their reason in AGENTS.md under project-specific overrides. `except Exception:` is permitted only at process boundaries (CLI main, HTTP handler) as a logging backstop.
